Title: Alvin Ch'ng turns substantial shareholder in Alam Maritim after Maybank exit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Alam Maritim Resources Bhd global subsea business head Alvin Ch'ng Yi Ming has emerged as a substantial shareholder following the recent exit of Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank).
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, Ch'ng acquired 39.70 million shares on March 19, representing an 8.91 per cent stake in the group.
This follows Maybank's disposal of its entire 19.16 per cent stake in Alam Maritim on the same day, which it had acquired through a debt settlement exercise in September 2025.
Separate filings showed that existing substantial shareholders have also increased their stakes.
Saujana Holdings Sdn Bhd raised its shareholding to 66.92 million shares, or 15.02 per cent, after a series of acquisitions between March 17 and March 24.
Meanwhile, global subsea business chief executive officer Datuk Aloysius Albert Michael increased his stake to 55.41 million shares, equivalent to a 12.44 per cent interest, after acquiring additional shares on March 19.
Maybank had previously emerged as a substantial shareholder in Alam Maritim after the group undertook a debt restructuring exercise, before exiting its position.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Denmark votes in close election, outgoing PM tipped to win
Article:
COPENHAGEN: Danes began voting on Tuesday in general elections, with Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen seen as the favourite after standing up to US President Donald Trump over Greenland.
The latest polls give the left-wing bloc, for which Frederiksen is the self-proclaimed candidate, a nine-seat lead over the right-wing bloc, but neither side is projected to win a majority of the 179 seats in Denmark's parliament, the Folketing.
Frederiksen, a Social Democrat who has been in office since 2019, has been praised for her leadership after fending off Trump's repeated demands to annex Greenland, a Danish autonomous territory he claims the United States needs for national security reasons.
At Copenhagen's City Hall, voters, many on their way to work, lined up under a cloudy sky to cast their ballots as polls opened at 8am (0700 GMT).
"The alternatives (to Mette Frederiksen) are worse," 24-year-old student Freja Strandlod told AFP just after casting her vote in central Copenhagen.
"People may not really like her, but they see her as the right leader," Elisabet Svane, political analyst at Danish newspaper Politiken, told AFP.
Frederiksen, who had "a prime minister you can count on" as one of her campaign slogans "is a unifying figure in a world full of insecurity, and Danes are quite anxious – there's Greenland, Ukraine, (and mystery) drones" that flew over the Scandinavian country last year, the Svane said.
In addition, "it's hard to imagine a right-wing government because it would have to unite such a broad swath, from the far right to the more centrist parties, which are not on very good terms with the far right," said Ole Waever, a political science professor at the University of Copenhagen.
The four overseas seats held by Denmark's two autonomous territories – two for Greenland and two for the Faroe Islands – could tip the balance if the election result is very close.
The centrist Moderate party, led by Foreign Minister Lars Lokke Rasmussen, a two-time former prime minister, could also prove decisive if things go down to the wire.
In Greenland's capital Nuuk, the campaign has generated more interest than usual, with more than 20 candidates standing.
"I think this election will kind of show us the direction going forward," said Juno Berthelsen, a member of Greenland's local parliament and leader of the Naleraq party, which wants to cut ties with Denmark as soon as possible.
[[nid:1402994]]
Several of the party's members have met with the Trump administration.
Greenland's main political parties all want independence from Denmark, but Naleraq's rivals favour a more measured process.
Greenland's Business Minister Naaja Nathanielsen, a candidate for the left-wing IA party, said fear of the United States had been central to the campaign in Greenland.
"Due to the fact that the US has shown such aggression, you would find a bigger interest in really trying to push the Greenlandic narrative in the Danish parliament," she said.
In metropolitan Denmark, the row over the vast Arctic island has however not been central in the campaign.
In the wealthy nation of six million people, the campaign has instead focused on domestic issues, including inflation, the welfare state and high nitrate levels in water from agriculture.
"During the campaign, there weren't really any key issues for me. I focused on climate policy and looked at the different parties to find the most pragmatic solution," said William, a 26-year-old trainee lawyer who did not want to give his last name.
In a country where the far right has heavily influenced policy since the late 1990s, immigration has also been a hot topic, with the Social Democrats advocating even tighter regulations.
Frederiksen has also defended as "fair" a proposal to deny non-essential health care to people of foreign origin who threaten medical personnel.
"It has to be this way: in addition to the formal punishment in the judicial system, we're saying, 'You have no place here'," she said.
Three populist parties are also in the running and opinion polls see them garnering around 19 per cent of the vote.
The most established of those is the far-right Danish People's Party, which slumped in the 2022 election but has seen an upswing in opinion polls.
In metropolitan Denmark, polling stations close at 8pm, with exit polls expected to be published just after.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fire, accident, breakdown slow traffic on major highways
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Traffic on several major highways slowed this afternoon following a vehicle fire, an accident and a lorry breakdown, according to updates from the Malaysian Highway Authority (MHA).
A car fire was reported at 3.33pm on the Damansara-Puchong Highway (LDP) at Km11.2 northbound from Putra Bend towards Taman Megah.
The bus bay slip lane was blocked, but traffic remained under control.
The MHA, via its official WhatsApp channel, also reported an accident at 3.38pm on the North-South Expressway (E2) at Km98.6 southbound from Yong Peng Utara to Yong Peng Selatan.
Traffic was moving slowly at the location.
Another lorry breakdown occurred at 3.59pm on the northbound stretch of the North-South Expressway (E1) at Km255.6 from Ipoh Utara towards Kuala Kangsar.
The left lane was obstructed, causing traffic to slow.
The MHA advised motorists to remain alert, follow instructions from personnel on site, and maintain a safe distance from other vehicles.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ultra-low-cost airlines made risky bet on fuel-efficient planes
Article:
ULTRA-low-cost carriers have some of the newest, most fuel-efficient planes in the industry, but if travel demand in the United States falters as a result of growing macroeconomic uncertainty, paying for the new planes could become a barrier to profit.
US carriers on Tuesday said that they were seeing stronger-than-expected spring travel demand.
Still, as the US-Israeli war on Iran has pushed US jet fuel prices above US$200 per barrel, legacy carriers are starting to trim capacity like United Airlines or could park aircraft to save on costs, a luxury that Frontier Airlines may not have.
Frontier said it saw an improvement in demand in the first quarter as it filled gaps left by Spirit's contraction on the West Coast.
The company's adjusted revenue per available seat mile, a proxy for pricing power, was trending higher before the fuel spike, a company spokesperson said.
Greater fuel efficiency is a clear advantage as airlines' largest expense after labour is jet fuel.
On Thursday, jet fuel surpassed US$200 per barrel, according to Kloza Advisors principal analyst Tom Kloza, compared to about US$105 before the conflict began.
Frontier started 2026 committed to raising its aircraft utilisation rate to cut costs and lower fares for customers.
Frontier said it has the most fuel-efficient fleet in the US and that its fuel burn per passenger is 40 per cent below its peers.
"We start from a better place on a per-passenger basis, given the high-density business model we have, and the predominance of new aircraft in the fleet," said James Dempsey, Frontier CEO, at the JP Morgan Industrials conference on Tuesday.
But low-cost carriers' success depends on keeping the planes moving.
The most fuel-efficient planes are the newest and most costly, and if demand starts to waver, it is financially tough to park them, said Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank research analyst in a note.
"It is far more 'expensive' for an airline to idle a new aircraft rather than an older one that is fully depreciated and financially unencumbered," Linenberg said, adding that if fuel prices more than double, operating an aircraft that is 15 per cent more efficient than the older generation does not even come close to offsetting the financial pain.
[[nid:1402982]]
Spirit, aiming to emerge from bankruptcy later this year, decided to return to lessors or sell most, possibly all, of its newer, more fuel-efficient Airbus A320neo aircraft, aviation industry analyst Henry Harteveldt said.
The decision reflects the higher ownership costs of these newer jets, he said, which were too expensive for the airline to retain in its fleet. Spirit declined to comment.
Frontier has also made moves to cut its fleet size, terminating the leases on 24 jets currently in service. It also deferred the induction of 69 A320neo jets, which were expected to be delivered between 2027 and 2030.
The Denver-based carrier's full-year fuel expenses fell 11 per cent to US$929 million in 2025 in part due to a 10 per cent decrease in fuel costs.
But Frontier's aircraft rent expense rose 11 per cent to US$748 million in the same period, as it brought on new aircraft.
A company spokesperson said Frontier was confident in its ability to weather the current volatile fuel environment.
Dempsey this week said that Frontier was "following suit" other airlines in raising fares.
As of Dec 2025, about 85 per cent of Frontier's fleet was the more fuel-efficient Airbus A320neo family aircraft.
Christopher Anderson, a professor at the Cornell SC Johnson College of Business, said carriers with newer fleets face pressure to generate sufficient cash flow to service those aircraft.
The issue is not new, added Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen equity analyst.
"Even prior to the fuel spike there was a dynamic where ultra-low-cost-carriers with newer planes were lowering utilisation and facing the headwind from parking expensive new assets," he said.
Newer planes also come with a higher maintenance bill, Fitzgerald said.
"The fuel efficiency gains of the new generation engines came at a price of durability."
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police hunt motorcyclist for dangerous firecracker stunt
Article:
SEGAMAT: Segamat police have launched a manhunt for a motorcyclist whose dangerous antics of lighting and throwing firecrackers while riding sparked outrage after a video of the incident went viral on social media.
The 15-second clip, which was widely circulated on WhatsApp and TikTok, showed a rider on a Yamaha RXZ performing a high-speed stunt while holding a lit firecracker.
Sparks were seen trailing from the rider's hand before the explosive was hurled onto the road, posing a serious risk to trailing motorists.
Segamat deputy police chief, Deputy Superintendent M. Nantha, confirmed that the incident was believed to have taken place at Km185 of Jalan Johor Baru–Seremban at 10.30pm last Sunday.
"Police viewed this blatant disregard for road safety and public order with extreme gravity. We were in the process of identifying and tracking down the suspect to facilitate further investigations," he said in a statement.
The stunt drew sharp criticism from the public, with many calling on authorities to impose the maximum penalty to deter others from similar dangerous acts for social media attention.
Nantha issued a warning that police would not hesitate to take firm action against any individual who flouted traffic laws and endangered the lives of others.
Members of the public with information on the rider or the vehicle's registration number were urged to contact the Segamat district police headquarters traffic police station at 07-9325811 or visit the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tel Aviv building damaged in 'direct' hit after Iran missile warning
Article:
TEL AVIV: The mayor of Tel Aviv said a "direct strike" damaged a building on Tuesday after an Iranian missile attack warning, as Israeli first responders said they had treated four people for light injuries.
The strike in an upscale neighbourhood in the north of the Israeli coastal city tore open the facade of an old three-storey building and scattered debris across the street, according to an AFP journalist at the scene.
Tel Aviv mayor Ron Huldai told reporters that the building had been hit by a "direct strike."
According to several Israeli media outlets, including military radio, police believe the damage was caused by a cluster munition missile equipped with three to four warheads, each carrying around 100 kilogrammes of explosives.
Israel's Magen David Adom emergency medical service released a video from one of the affected apartments that showed extensive damage from the explosion.
MDA said it had treated and quickly discharged four people, revising down an earlier toll of six people lightly wounded.
A source at the MDA said the individuals had inhaled smoke following a small fire caused by the explosion.
The Israeli military had so far issued on Tuesday seven warnings of incoming missile fire from Iran in central and southern Israel, as well as at least six warnings of missiles or rockets fired from Lebanon in the north.
Israel's military has said that it intercepts just over 90 per cent of incoming fire from Iran and regularly reminds residents to take shelter during alerts, as missile defence is not completely "airtight."
Police said they had deployed bomb disposal teams to "several impact sites of munitions" in the Tel Aviv district, but no injuries were reported beyond the four people treated following the direct strike on the residential building.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTViral: Police chase ends in crash through Raya tent in Kulim [WATCH]
Article:
KULIM: A police pursuit took a dramatic turn when a fleeing Perodua Kancil crashed through a tent outside a house in Padang Serai on the third day of Hari Raya Aidilfitri.
The incident, believed to have occurred around 10pm, was captured on closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage and has since circulated on social media.
In the clip, sirens can be heard as the driver speeds through a residential area while attempting to evade police. The car is then seen ploughing through the tent before continuing its escape.
Kulim deputy police chief Tengku Mohd Faisal Tengku Yeng confirmed the incident, adding that no injuries were reported.
He said the suspect had earlier collided with another vehicle while trying to flee.
Police are continuing investigations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: LLM: 2.8 million vehicles expected on highways today
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Highway Authority (LLM) expects some 2.8 million vehicles to flood major highways today as commuters returned to the city centre following the Aidilfitri holidays.
Traffic Management Centre supervisor Muhammad Aliff Syafiq Md Takiyudin said the figure represented the highest traffic volume recorded since the start of the festive season.
Heavy traffic was reported on the North-South Expressway (NSE) between Permatang Pauh and Perai, as well as along the stretch from Kuala Kangsar towards Ipoh.
Congestion was also building up for those travelling from the south, particularly between Ayer Hitam and Yong Peng, and from Simpang Empat towards Pedas Linggi.
On the Kuala Lumpur-Karak Expressway, traffic volume increased significantly from Bentong heading towards Bukit Tinggi as east coast travellers returned to the Klang Valley.
While no major accidents or breakdowns disrupted traffic flow, 21 smart lanes were activated as at 10am today to help ease congestion.
LLM advised motorists travelling from the north to consider using the West Coast Expressway (WCE) between Taiping Utara and Hutan Melintang as an alternative route.
Those returning from the east coast were encouraged to utilise federal roads to avoid heavy congestion on main highways.
Road users were urged to plan their journeys by referring to the Travel Time Advisory (TTA) via the MyPlus-TTA app or the AFA Prime website.
For the latest updates or enquiries, the public can contact the LLM toll-free hotline at 1-800-88-7752.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Traffic crawls on major expressways after bushfire, vehicle breakdowns
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Motorists across several major highways faced delays today after a string of incidents, including a bushfire, vehicle breakdowns, and a road accident, disrupted traffic flow.
A bushfire at 11.58am near KM109.7 of the North-South Expressway (E1), on the northbound stretch from Bertam to Sungai Petani Selatan, caused early congestion, with drivers urged to follow instructions from personnel on site.
The Malaysian Highway Authority (MHA), on its official WhatsApp channel, said a stalled lorry at the Bukit Tagar interchange, heading towards Bukit Beruntung, blocked the left lane at 12.52pm. The obstruction was cleared and traffic brought under control by 2.26pm.
Another lorry breakdown at 1.10pm at KM208 of the E2 highway, on the northbound route from Ayer Keroh to Simpang Ampat, also led to slow-moving traffic after the left lane was blocked.
On the Kuala Lumpur–Karak Highway, an accident at 2.46pm near KM66.6 on the westbound stretch from Karak to Bentong blocked the emergency lane and caused traffic to crawl.
A separate fire was reported at 3.21pm on the Maju Expressway (Mex) at KM21.6 southbound from Seri Kembangan towards Putrajaya, where the left lane was obstructed. Traffic remained under control.
Despite generally smooth conditions earlier in the afternoon, high traffic volume led to congestion on several stretches of the E1 southbound, including between Kepala Batas and Hutan Kampung, and from Kuala Kangsar to the Menora Tunnel.
Similar delays were reported on the E2, with northbound traffic slow between Machap and Ayer Hitam, and southbound congestion from Simpang Renggam to Sedenak.
In the north, traffic was also slow on the E1 from Sungai Dua towards the Sungai Dua toll plaza, and from Gurun to Sungai Petani Selatan.
By contrast, traffic on the Plus E6 (Elite) highway between Putrajaya and Bandar Saujana Putra remained smooth in both directions as of 1.53pm, under fair weather conditions.
The MHA has urged motorists to remain alert, keep a safe distance, and signal when changing lanes to ensure safer journeys.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EPF, SP Setia deny Battersea allegations by former CEO
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Employees Provident Fund (EPF) and SP Setia Bhd have firmly denied allegations made by a former chief executive officer of London's Battersea Power Station, stating they are unfounded and are being robustly contested.
In separate statements, both parties clarified that there are no claims by Don O'Sullivan relating to the accounts of Battersea Power Station Development Company (BPSDC).
The EPF emphasised that it is not a party to the ongoing Employment Tribunal proceedings and reiterated its commitment to upholding all applicable laws, governance standards and fiduciary responsibilities across its investments.
Similarly, SP Setia said it has consistently acted in accordance with regulatory requirements and governance principles.
"The Financial Times article referred to an ongoing legal claim, and we are mindful of the confidential nature of Employment Tribunal procedures in England and Wales. We cannot go into further details, not least because S P Setia Bhd is not a party to any proceedings. We believe the legal process must take its course," the property company said
Battersea Power Station (BPS) Development Ltd is a wholly owned subsidiary of BPS Holdings, a Jersey-registered entity owned 40 per cent each by Sime Darby Property Bhd and SP Setia Bhd, with the remaining 20 per cent held by the Employees Provident Fund.
BPSDC manages the Battersea Power Station estate ultimately on behalf of Battersea Project Holding Company, the holding company of the 42-acre regeneration project
O'Sullivan, who joined BPSDC in June 2024, claims he was dismissed in May 2025 after raising internal concerns about certain matters at the company.
According to a statement today issued on his behalf, he alleges that the dismissal followed accusations of gross misconduct, which he disputes, and that the issues he raised included alleged financial misreporting.
"O'Sullivan's case contends that the effect of the financial misreporting has been to grossly (and falsely) flatter the balance sheet of BPS Holdings. His concerns have been verified by an external forensic accounting report prepared by Moore Kingston Smith," the statement said.
It added that O'Sullivan, a seasoned residential and commercial developer known for his tenure as CEO of Galliard Homes, was dismissed from the company after reporting his concerns in November 2024.
According to the statement, the matters flagged by O'Sullivan were discussed at a board meeting in London on Dec 16, 2024. He claims he was excluded from company activities the following day and subsequently suspended later that month.
The case is currently before the London South Employment Tribunal and is ongoing. A final hearing has been scheduled for 2029, reflecting broader delays within the tribunal system. No findings have been made at this stage, and all allegations remain unproven pending the outcome of the proceedings.
O'Sullivan is represented by legal counsel from Constantine Law and Nine Chambers, while Battersea Power Station Development Company is represented by Brown Rudnick and counsel from 11 KBW.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Sulking cat over ketupat and rendang steals hearts online [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A cat's adorable "sulking" behaviour after being denied festive food has amused netizens, with a TikTok video garnering more than 1.1 million views.
The video, posted by TikTok user @ddraz.musa, showed a ginger cat turning its back and pressing its face against a chair, as if refusing to acknowledge its owner.
In the clip, the owner said that the cat, named Simba, was upset after not being given ketupat and rendang ayam during Hari Raya Aidilfitri.
"I didn't let him eat ketupat and chicken. It might upset his stomach, but he still wants it," he said, expressing both concern and amusement.
The video quickly drew attention online, with many netizens entertained by Simba's human-like reaction.
@drraz.musa
#fyp
♬ original sound - RazMus - ~Muhammad Razee Musa
"Oh my God, you are so adorable. It's my first time seeing a cat sulk like this just because it didn't get ketupat and rendang," a user commented.
Another TikTok user wrote: "My cat also likes ketupat and rendang."
A third user shared a similar experience, saying their cat would steal ketupat daily from their mother, who sells the festive delicacy.
Meanwhile, some netizens jokingly sympathised with the cat, suggesting it should be allowed to "celebrate Raya" as well.
"Just give it to him, he's tired of fasting, he also wants to celebrate Raya," one user joked.
Others, however, reminded viewers that such festive foods may not be suitable for pets.
"Don't eat ketupat, kitty. Your fur will fall out, you won't look nice anymore," another user commented.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Raya traffic woes: When a five-hour trip turns into 14
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: What should have been a five-hour journey from Kedah to Kuala Lumpur turned into a 14-hour crawl, as one motorist recounted the toll of festive traffic and reminded of the need for better preparation for long drives.
Siti Mariam said her trip from Pokok Sena, Kedah, to Setapak quickly descended into gridlock after she exited the Alor Setar Utara toll plaza and joined the North-South Expressway.
"The jam started getting bad almost immediately. For most of the journey, I was moving at just 20 to 30km/h. The fastest I reached was 80km/h, and even that didn't last," she said.
Despite driving an automatic car, she said the constant stop-and-go traffic left her with aching knees by the time she arrived.
But she described fatigue as the biggest danger during the journey.
"I was so sleepy, but the road was too packed for me to even pull over for a drink," she said, adding that when she eventually entered a rest and relaxation (R&R) stop, she had to circle three times before finding a parking space.
The congestion persisted despite the activation of "smart lanes", with traffic barely moving due to the volume of vehicles.
Her experience was echoed by another driver, who said his journey from Padang Terap to Jalan Klang Lama took 10 hours.
"I left at 5pm and arrived at 3am. Traffic was heavy through Kedah, Penang, Perak and Selangor, and only eased near Kuala Lumpur," he said, noting there were no major accidents along the route.
Reflecting on her experience, Siti reminded Malaysians to plan ahead before travelling during peak periods.
"Please plan your journey properly. Keep food and coffee in the car, and make sure you get enough rest before driving. You need to stay alert for the long haul," she said.
Authorities have introduced measures such as real-time WhatsApp alerts and Travel Time Advisory (TTA) updates via the Malaysian Highway Authority and Plus Malaysia Berhad to help motorists better manage their journeys during peak travel periods.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar discusses developments in Middle East conflict with NZ PM
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: In a call with New Zealand Prime Minister Christopher Luxon, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim stressed Malaysia's strong condemnation of the military attack on Iran, emphasising the country's firm rejection of any unilateral actions that violate international law.
In a Facebook post today, Anwar said he and Luxon discussed developments in the Middle East and their implications for global security and the economy.
"Malaysia continues to call for an immediate ceasefire, the protection of civilians and for all parties to return to the negotiating table through diplomatic channels."
He said he and Luxon also discussed the impact of the conflict on New Zealand's energy sector, including rising fuel prices and pressure on the domestic economy.
"I welcomed the proactive steps taken by the New Zealand government to strengthen energy security and accelerate the transition to renewable energy."
He said both leaders reaffirmed their commitment to further strengthening bilateral ties under the strategic partnership framework.
"This includes cooperation in the areas of trade, investment and energy. We will maintain close communication to address current challenges in the interest of our mutual benefit."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Philippines warns oil price hike could send inflation soaring, slow growth
Article:
Reuters
MANILA: The Philippines is preparing for a range of possible impacts from rising oil prices, including a worst-case scenario that could push inflation into double-digit territory and pull economic growth below target this year.
Before the conflict in the Middle East, the Philippines had expected inflation to accelerate to 3.6 per cent this year from 1.7 per cent in 2025, still within its two to four per cent target, and for the economy to expand five to six per cent from 4.4 per cent last year.
In a presentation to the Senate on Tuesday, Economic Planning Secretary Arsenio Balisacan outlined five scenarios of varying severity based on different oil price levels and the duration in which those prices held.
Under the most severe scenario, one where oil prices reach US$200 per barrel and stay at that level for six months, inflation could surge sharply, reaching 7.3 per cent to 8.6 per cent for the full year, and hitting as high as 14.3 per cent at one point.
Under that scenario, the likelihood of which Balisacan said was "quite low", economic growth could be reduced by 1.47 to 1.95 percentage points to 3.5 per cent to 4.0 per cent.
"This scenario is really a bit scary if it happens because it could bring us to double-digit inflation which we never had in the last couple of years," Balisacan said.
Even in the least severe scenario, where oil stays at US$100 a barrel for a month, inflation could exceed four per cent, while growth could fall by less than a quarter of a percentage point from the government's 5.5 per cent baseline, Balisacan said.
Oil prices held above US$100 a barrel on Tuesday, and Iran has threatened to send oil prices to US$200 a barrel.
The Philippines relies heavily on imported fuel for its energy requirements. Energy Secretary Sharon Garin on Tuesday said the Philippines has manageable fuel supply but was working to procure one million barrels of oil within and outside Southeast Asia.
She said the Philippines had been assured by sources like South Korea, China, of continued supply.
But Garin told the same Senate hearing that "uncertainty will always be there in the next round of orders."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israeli minister calls for annexation of southern Lebanon
Article:
BEIRUT:  Israel should extend its border with Lebanon up to the Litani River deep inside the country's south, Israel's finance minister said on Monday as Israeli troops bombed bridges and destroyed homes in an escalating military assault.
The comments by Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich were the most explicit yet by a senior Israeli official on seizing Lebanese territory in a fight Israel says targets Iran-backed Hizbollah fighters.
Lebanon was pulled into the regional war on March 2 when Hizbollah fired missiles into Israel.
Since then, Israel has ordered all residents to evacuate south of the Litani River as it pummels the area with air strikes, viewing it as a stronghold of Hizbollah, which has kept up rocket attacks on Israel.
Lebanese authorities say the Israeli air and ground assault has killed more than 1,000 people, and more than a million have been driven from their homes.
Reuters witnesses heard at least three blasts in Beirut's Dahiyeh district late on Monday, as the Israeli military said it had attacked Hizbollah sites in the city.
An earlier strike in Beirut on Monday killed a commander of the elite Quds Force of Iran's Revolutionary Guards, the Israeli military said.
Smotrich told an Israeli radio programme that the military campaign in Lebanon "needs to end with a different reality entirely, both with the Hizbollah decision but also with the change of Israel's borders."
"I say here definitively...in every room and in every discussion, too: the new Israeli border must be the Litani," Smotrich said.
A military official told Reuters on Monday he couldn't comment about politicians' remarks or government's long-term plans, but that the Israeli ground troops were limiting their raids to areas near the border.
Smotrich, leader of a small far-right party in Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's cabinet, has made similar comments about Gaza, which have oftengone beyond official Israeli policy.
Netanyahu's office did not respond to a request for comment. Defence Minister Israel Katz said earlier this month that Lebanon could face "loss of territory" if it did not disarm Hizbollah.
Smotrich's remarks were deeply resonant in Lebanon, which is trying to emerge from a decades-old cycle of invasions and occupations by its neighbour. Israeli forces have launched repeated assaults on Lebanon since 1978 and occupied the south from 1982-2000.
A Lebanese official told Reuters that Beirut was still counting on foreign powers to put enough pressure on Israel to put an end to the war, through an offer from President Joseph Aoun to hold direct talks.
The Israeli military says ⁠its troops in Lebanon are carrying out ground maneuvers and raids on Hizbollah fighters and weapons stores, aimed at protecting residents in northern Israel from Hizbollah.
On Monday, its troops captured "several" Hizbollah fighters south of the Litani, the military said. Hizbollah was not immediately reachable for comment.
The Lebanese government has outlawed Hizbollah military activity and said it wants to engage ​in direct talks with Israel.
Over the weekend, Israel struck a main bridge linking south Lebanon with the rest of the country after ordering its military to destroy all crossings over the Litani ‌River and to step up the demolition of homes near the southern border.
International law generally prohibits militaries from attacking civilian infrastructure, and the United Nations human rights chief has criticised Israel's actions in Lebanon, particularly its use of widespread ​evacuation orders.
Israel says the evacuations are meant to keep civilians out of harm's way when it attacks Hizbollah.
On Monday, it issued evacuation orders for seven neighbourhoods in Beirut's southern suburbs and said it will continue striking Hizbollah with "increasing force."
Israeli strikes hit two more crossings on the Litani River on Monday – a road running near a main bridge hit on Sunday and another small bridge on another section of the river.
Hanna Amil, the mayor of Christian border town Rmeish, whose residents have refused to leave their homes, told Reuters that it was getting increasingly difficult to move around.
"Once or twice a week, a convoy from the Lebanese army accompanies us as we try to get basic goods from nearby areas," he said.
"Already, we have no state electricity, no water and we have diesel shortages. If all the routes to the north get cut off, who knows what the future could hold for us," Amil said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman, 2 kids die in accident near Dungun, 2 other kids survive
Article:
DUNGUN: A woman and her two children were killed in an accident near Felda Jerangau here yesterday after attending a wedding.
Nurul Hafizah Mohd Shaary, 39, and her son, Muhammad Redzuan Arash Mohamad Redzuan, 13, died at the scene of the incident at Km59 of Jalan Jerangau-Jabor.
Her daughter, Nur Radya Athalia, 4, died while receiving emergency treatment at the Jerangau Health Clinic.
Nurul Hafizah's two other children, Nur Rakhis Aral, 9, and Nur Raesha Amanda, 14, were injured and taken to Dungun Hospital.
Nurul Hafizah's brother, Mohd Saddam Mohd Shaary, 33, said the family was shocked by the incident as it happened so soon after they gathered for their youngest sister's wedding in Kampung Sura Tengah.
"During the wedding, my sister was very happy and cheerful. She kept joking with family members, which was different from usual.
"About 5.30pm, my sister and her four children gave us 'salam' then they left for home."
He said about 30 minutes later, he received a call from a family member informing him that his sister had been involved in an accident.
He said he did not go to the scene as his brother-in-law, Mohamad Redzuan, had already gone there, so he went to Dungun Hospital to look after his two injured nieces.
Nur Rakhis Aral suffered an injury to the left arm. She was discharged from Dungun Hospital this morning.
Nur Raesha Amanda suffered rib injuries. She was transferred to Kemaman Hospital for further treatment and is in stable condition.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UN Security Council not likely to approve use of force in Strait of Hormuz
Article:
PARIS: Bahrain has put forward a draft United Nations Security Council resolution that would authorise countries to use "all necessary means" - diplomatic language for force - to protect commercial shipping in and around the Strait of Hormuz, according to a text seen by Reuters on Monday.
Diplomats said the draft text was backed by other Gulf Arab states and the United States, although they said it was unlikely to succeed.
The move underscores mounting concern in the region that Iran could continue to threaten the Strait of Hormuz, a strategic chokepoint that carries about a fifth of global oil supplies and underpins Gulf economies.
Closing the Strait has been one of Iran's main objectives. Shipping through the waterway has ground to a near-⁠halt after Iran hit vessels in its conflict with the US and Israel.
The draft resolution calls Iran's actions a threat to international peace and security.
The text would authorise countries, acting alone or through voluntary multinational naval coalitions, to use "all necessary means" in and around the Strait of Hormuz – including in the territorial waters of littoral states – to ensure passage and to prevent moves that block or interfere with international navigation.
The resolution also expresses the readiness to impose measures, including targeted sanctions.
The Bahraini and US missions at the United Nations did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
The draft text "demands that the Islamic Republic of Iran immediately cease all attacks against merchant and commercial vessels and any attempt to impede lawful transit passage or freedom of navigation in and around the Strait of Hormuz."
Two European and one Western diplomat said there was little prospect of such a resolution being adopted by the Security Council as Iran's allies Russia and China were likely to veto the text if needed.
A resolution needs at least nine votes in favour and no vetoes by Russia, China, the US, Britain and France to be adopted by the 15-member body. The Russian and Chinese missions to the United Nations were not immediately available for comment.
However, the diplomats said France was also working on an alternative draft resolution that would seek a UN mandate once the situation had calmed.
Three US officials have told Reuters that 2,500 Marines, along with the USS Boxer, an amphibious assault ship, and accompanying warships would deploy to the region, although they did not say what their role would be.
Two officials said there had been no decision on whether to send troops into Iran itself. Sources previously told Reuters that possible targets could include Iran's coast or Kharg Island oil export hub.
The resolution would be placed under Chapter Seven of the UN Charter, which allows the council to authorise actions ranging from sanctions to the use of force.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil swings back above US$100 amid conflicting US-Iran signals
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Brent crude has rebounded above US$100 a barrel, a day after plunging more than 10 per cent, amid conflicting signals over potential talks between the United States and Iran.
Global energy markets have remained volatile since the US–Israel alliance launched what was described as a "preemptive" attack on Iran on Feb 28. At press time, Brent crude rose 2.67 per cent to US$102.60 a barrel.
On Monday, the oil benchmark fell as much as 14.37 per cent, sliding from an intraday high of US$114.11 to a low of US$97.71, after US President Donald Trump said Washington had engaged in negotiations with Tehran and delayed a threatened strike on Iranian power plants.
The development lifted Wall Street, with the three major US stock indexes closing more than one per cent higher.
However, Iran's parliamentary speaker later said no talks had taken place with the US, contradicting Trump's claim.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New species of marine copepod found in Sipadan's Turtle Tomb
Article:
SEMPORNA: A new species of marine copepod has been discovered in waters off Pulau Sipadan, adding to understanding of the area's rich marine biodiversity.
The 'Peltidium penyu' was identified by a research team led by Associate Professor Dr Azman Abdul Rahim of Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia during a marine biodiversity survey.
The survey was part of the global Ocean Census initiative aimed at accelerating the discovery and documentation of marine life.
The specimen was collected from an underwater cave system known as the Turtle Tomb, where a lot of skeletal remains of sea turtles can be found. Researchers found the specimen using a light trap near the remains.
Azman said a distinct feature of the 'Peltidium penyu' is its carapace structure, which looks like a sea turtle's shell.
Images of the organism have drawn attention from researchers and the public after they surfaced online.
Following detailed morphological analysis and field validation, the team confirmed it as a new species within the 'Peltidium' genus.
The findings were published in the Raffles Bulletin of Zoology.
This discovery underscores the importance of continued marine exploration, particularly in Southeast Asia's lesser-studied habitats.
"This discovery showcases not only the hidden wonders of our marine ecosystems, but also the value of community involvement and passive citizen science in contributing to meaningful scientific breakthroughs," said Azman.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Padang Serai shooting: Suspect with 13 prior convictions held within 24 hours
Article:
KULIM: A 42-year-old lorry driver arrested over a shooting in Padang Serai has 13 previous convictions linked to crime and drugs, police said.
Kulim deputy police chief, Deputy Superintendent Tengku Mohd Faisal Tengku Yeng, said the suspect was detained less than 24 hours after the incident, about 12.30am, in a house in Kampung Naga Lilit.
Police also seized a .38-calibre revolver, 10 rounds of ammunition with casings, a yellow Honda Civic and a mobile phone.
"Checks found the suspect, a local man, has 13 prior convictions related to crime and drugs. However, he tested negative for drugs," he told a press conference at the Kulim district police headquarters.
The suspect has been remanded for six days until March 26. Police are still investigating the motive.
Preliminary findings indicate the suspect and the victim knew each other. In the early hours of March 21, the 32-year-old victim was reportedly heading to his mother's house in Kampung Permatang Durian
when the incident occurred.
"Witnesses said an argument broke out before the suspect fired a shot, hitting the victim in the right arm and chest, before fleeing the scene," he said.
The victim, a factory operator, is being treated at Kulim Hospital and is in stable condition.
"Initial medical reports show the gunshot did not hit any critical internal organs. Checks also found the victim has 12 prior criminal and drug-related offences," he added.
The case is being investigated under Section 307 of the Penal Code and Section 3 of the Firearms (Increased Penalties) Act 1971.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL ordered to pay RM824,180 over fatal falling tree incident
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Sessions Court here has ordered the Kuala Lumpur Mayor to pay RM824,180 in damages to a couple who lost their son after he was killed by a fallen tree on a road divider maintained by Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL).
Judge Effandi Nazila Abdullah made the ruling after allowing the claim filed by Gooi Ling Sim, 61, and Phun Kok Hoi, 63, as dependants of the late Phun Mun Lap, 30, against the Kuala Lumpur Mayor as the defendant.
The court awarded RM26,180 for funeral expenses, RM768,000 for loss of dependency, and RM30,000 in bereavement damages to the couple.
In his judgment dated March 9, Effandi ruled that the fallen tree was under DBKL's care and that the accident would not have occurred without negligence in monitoring and maintenance.
"The tree that fell was under DBKL's maintenance, and its responsibility is therefore clear and established. The defendant also admitted that the tree was excessively large and had not been properly maintained," the judge said.
The court further found that DBKL failed to produce any documentary evidence showing that maintenance or monitoring works, such as cutting, trimming, or pruning, had been carried out on the tree.
In addition, the court noted that the defendant did not dispute that the tree was under its maintenance, a position supported by the defendant's own witness.
The judge also held that DBKL had failed to discharge its statutory duty and duty of care in carrying out maintenance, inspection, prevention, and control measures to ensure that trees along road dividers are safe and do not pose a danger to road users.
"The defendant's witness also acknowledged that DBKL has a statutory duty under the Local Government Act 1976 to maintain and monitor trees planted within its jurisdiction," he said.
The trial was held over two days in September 2025, during which four witnesses for both the plaintiffs and the defendant were called to testify.
On March 22, 2023, the victim, who was riding a motorcycle to work along Jalan Kuching here, was struck by a fallen tree from a road divider near the DBKL gateway, close to the Selayang market.
The victim, a crane driver, sustained severe injuries and was treated at Selayang Hospital before being pronounced dead on March 24, 2023.
The victim's parents pleaded that the accident was caused by DBKL's negligence, including its failure to ensure that trees planted along the road divider were safe, its failure to carry out proper maintenance and regular inspections, and its failure to take adequate preventive measures.
They further claimed that DBKL failed to select suitable tree species for planting and did not provide sufficient safety distance or buffer zones between the trees and road users.
DBKL has appealed against the decision to the High Court.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Musk's Starlink denied telecoms licence in Namibia
Article:
WINDHOEK: Namibia has turned down applications from Elon Musk's satellite internet provider Starlink for a telecommunications service licence and access to radio spectrum, a notice in the Southern African country's government gazette showed.
The notice dated March 23 did not say why Starlink's applications were declined.
The Communications Regulatory Authority of Namibia could reconsider the decisions "on its own motion or on a petition filed by an aggrieved party" within 90 days, the notice added.
A spokesperson for the regulator said a statement would be issued later.
SpaceX, parent company of Starlink, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Starlink operates in several African countries but has faced regulatory challenges in others and resistance from state telecoms monopolies.
In Nov 2024, the Namibian regulator hit Starlink with a cease-and-desist order, saying it had been operating in the country without a licence.
It also warned consumers against purchasing Starlink equipment and said it had confiscated illegal terminals from consumers. SpaceX did not respond to a request for comment at the time.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NGOs seek update on MACC probe into DoE top officials over e-waste scandal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Two NGOs have urged the government to provide updates on the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission's (MACC) investigation into alleged corruption involving the Department of Environment (DoE) director-general and his deputy.
Sahabat Alam Malaysia (SAM) president Meenakshi Raman and Centre to Combat Corruption and Cronyism (C4) chief executive officer Pushpan Murugiah said there have been no updates since MACC submitted investigation papers to the deputy public prosecutor earlier this month with a recommendation for formal charges.
"However, since then, no further details have been made transparent, and it is not clear whether the DoE's top officials have been suspended pending the formal charging of the officials concerned.
"Allowing them to continue to be in office raises serious issues over evidence tampering and compromise prosecution efforts," they said in a joint statement today.
The NGOs called on MACC and the Attorney-General's Chambers (A-GC) to disclose the current status of the case, including when the officials are expected to be charged.
They also urged the Natural Resources and Environment Ministry to clarify what administrative measures have been put in place to prevent conflicts of interest or further abuse of power while investigations continue.
"Regular updates must be provided to the public, ensuring transparency and reinforcing confidence in Malaysia's anti-corruption and environmental protection institutions.
"Malaysia's environmental leadership cannot be compromised by opacity or delay.
"The public deserves clarity on how corruption allegations at the highest levels of the DoE are being addressed," the statement read.
SAM and C4 called on MACC, A-GC and the ministry to act decisively, transparently, and in full accordance with principles of good governance and environmental justice.
On Jan 29, the director-general and his deputy were remanded for seven days to assist in a
corruption investigation
involving electronic waste management.
The investigation focuses on allegations of abuse of power and bribery linked to e-waste management, suspected to have occurred over several years.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Modern beggars' amass easy wealth through social media donation scams
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Thousands of Malaysians are believed to be amassing easy wealth by acting as "modern beggars," soliciting public donations through live-streaming sessions on social media platforms.
The threat of financial misappropriation by these individuals is becoming rampant, with live sessions purportedly held to aid the poor or war victims often serving as a front to funnel contributions into personal pockets.
The activity involves both individuals and non-governmental organisations (NGOs) that successfully collect vast sums.
A lack of transparency in fund management has allowed these groups to create extraordinary wealth quickly through misappropriation.
Exploiting the public's desire to perform charitable acts, these solicitors often use emotional narratives and religious sentiments to drive donations.
Some "donation scammers" have even established networks on social media, posing as influencers or heads of charitable bodies.
Pertubuhan Mesra Pengguna Malaysia (PMPM) deputy president Azlin Othman said this trend is alarming as it is difficult for the public to distinguish between genuine cases and scams.
"Social media allows anyone to plead for money without transparency or accountability. They sell emotional narratives that go viral to gain sympathy before providing a platform for quick donations.
"The actual amount collected is never disclosed, nor is there a report on whether the money reached the intended recipients," she told Berita Harian.
It was previously reported that the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is proposing three new laws, the Misconduct in Public Office Act, the Charities Act, and the Political Funding Act, to close legal loopholes that allow grand corruption and fund misappropriation.
Its chief commissioner, Tan Sri Azam Baki, had said these specific laws are necessary to prevent the loss of public funds involving massive values.
Currently, the MACC is investigating more than five NGOs over the management of public funds, including organisations collecting donations in the name of Palestine.
Azlin said the generous nature of Malaysians allows scammers to rake in millions of ringgit in a short period through a single social media post.
She suggested that authorities monitor influencers with large followings who have the potential to collect significant sums.
"TikTok has been identified as a primary medium for these activities. Tactics include displaying personal bank account QR codes during live sessions while claiming the funds are for cancer patients, mosques, or tahfiz centres.
"Some sessions attract up to 10,000 viewers, allowing collectors to obtain thousands of ringgit in minutes. In some cases, scammers display QR codes under institutional names that actually link to private accounts."
One victim, Arsyad Abdullah, 25, said he was duped by an individual claiming to need funds for a pet's medical treatment.
"I contributed after hearing the sad story, but later found out it was a scam after other users reported the same modus operandi," he said.
A celebrity preacher, identified only as Ali, also revealed he was a victim of identity theft. Scammers used artificial intelligence (AI) to edit his photos and videos to solicit donations for a madrasah.
"I understand it was generated using AI. I have lodged a police report as this has tarnished my image," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fireworks stall blaze: Probe ongoing, multiple angles under scrutiny
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: Authorities are examining multiple angles in the investigation into a fire that razed a fireworks stall in Taman Pekaka here on Sunday.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department director Mohamad Shoki Hamzah said the forensic unit is conducting a detailed probe to determine the cause of the blaze.
"Investigations are still ongoing by our forensic unit, and we are looking at the case from all aspects," he told the New Straits Times.
Shoki said several samples have been collected from the scene and sent for laboratory testing.
"We have gathered several samples and submitted them for lab testing," he added.
Shoki said the investigation is expected to be completed within 14 days, although findings may be released earlier if ready.
"The investigation will be completed within 14 days.
"If it is ready earlier, we will release the findings immediately," he said.
The case is being investigated under Section 286 of the Penal Code for negligent conduct involving explosive substances.
Police have received a total of five reports in connection with the incident.
In the midnight incident on Sunday, the stall was razed in a blaze that also caused 90 per cent damage.
No casualties were reported.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail confirmed three caretakers of the stall, including a woman, have been detained to assist in the probe.
Police have also recorded a statement from the owner of the stall, who was not at the scene when the
incident
occurred.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ecological sustainability helps propel the data centre boom
Article:
LETTER:
EVERY time you stream a video, scroll social media, or use AI, your data travels through a data centre.
These facilities are the unseen heroes of our digital lives. In Malaysia, they are our visible neighbours.
With 51 data centre projects approved in Johor alone — 17 already operational, 11 under construction, and 23 approved just in 2025 — many are asking: Is it safe to live near one?
The short answer is yes, with proper regulations. This is where Malaysia's story becomes truly significant. Data centres are rated Tier I to IV based on their reliability and redundancy.
This classification has a massive side-effect on sustainability. Lower-tier facilities (Tiers I and II) are incredibly inefficient with water usage.
They can consume a staggering 40 to 50 million litres of water per day, which is enough to supply over 300,000 households.
A modern, higher-tier facility (Tiers III and IV) uses 200,000 litres per day.
This means lower-tier centres can use 200 times more water than their higher-tier counterparts.
Recognising this, the Johor state government has announced that it will no longer approve new Tier 1 and Tier 2 data centres.
Mandating higher-tier facilities ensures new data centres are more sustainable, meeting strict water usage effectiveness targets.
The national guideline recommends a design WUE (Water Usage Effectiveness) of 2.2 cubic metres per megawatt-hour or lower, avoiding a massive strain on the public water supply.
Furthermore, new applications are now vetted by a committee of 20 agencies, reviewing everything from water and power usage to cooling technology and green initiatives.
For noise control, cantilevered walls that channel sound upward can achieve significant noise reduction.
Sound-absorbing panels on existing walls act like giant mufflers, and full or partial enclosures for loud equipment like cooling towers can reduce noise by over 20 decibels.
For electromagnetic fields (EMF) mitigation, shielded cabling and proper grounding of all equipment, along with equipment segregation, ensure exposure remains minimal.
The issues of noise, air pollution and water usage are being increasingly addressed by Malaysia's evolving regulatory framework.
Johor's policy ensures that future data centres will be Tier III or IV, which are not only more reliable for the digital economy but are also significantly better neighbours for the community and the environment.
The global data centre boom is real, with Malaysia's RM113.8 billion investment in this sector proving it.
PHILLIP M.R.
Seremban,
Negri Sembilan
*The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police step in as broken-down car adds to post-Raya traffic snarl
Article:
TAMPARULI: Two traffic police officers helped ease congestion along a busy stretch of Jalan Tamparuli–Ranau after assisting an elderly couple whose vehicle broke down during peak holiday traffic.
The incident, which occurred at 2.23pm near Tamparuli Point, came as roads in the district grew increasingly congested with motorists returning to work after the Aidilfitri break.
The Tuaran district police headquarters (IPD) said the officers, Sergeant Larry Gurom Anak Nelson Atang and Sergeant Ridely Tamping, were on duty managing traffic when they spotted the stranded vehicle.
Acting quickly, Sergeant Larry helped move the car to a safer location to reduce the risk of an accident and restore traffic flow along the main route.
"The actions of the traffic police reflect the police's commitment to assisting the public and ensuring the safety of road users," the statement said.
Traffic in the area has been heavy since yesterday, particularly along routes linking Kota Kinabalu, Kota Belud, Kota Marudu and Kudat, as well as the Kota Kinabalu–Tamparuli–Ranau road.
Police said officers remain on the ground to manage traffic and minimise disruption as holidaymakers make their way back.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia as an oil producer: A half-told story, give the government room
Article:
FOR years, the argument that "Malaysia is an oil producer" has been used to justify why pump prices must remain low in Malaysia, by both the government and the public.
Regardless of global oil prices, fuel prices in Malaysia (currently subsidised for citizens) are expected to remain cheap.
So how do pump prices stay low even when global prices rise? For the public, that is the government's responsibility. The government 'must' absorb the difference between pump prices and actual market prices by increasing subsidies.
That is the general expectation. For years, we have been fed with a half-told story. Malaysia is an oil producer, so when global prices rise, the country must be earning more. Isn't Petronas owned by the government? Return the increased revenue to the people, increase subsidies!
Few would dispute that the US/Israel–Iran conflict has severely disrupted global oil supply. The geography of global oil supply is unique. About 30 per cent of the world's oil supply passes through the Strait of Hormuz, which at its narrowest point is only about 39km wide — roughly the distance between Bangi and Seremban — and is partly controlled by Iran.
The oil-producing regions are now unstable. Crude oil prices surged from about US$70 per barrel before the conflict to as high as US$112, a 60 per cent increase within just a few weeks. Prices remain elevated at around US$104.
Careful assessment and preparation are crucial. Higher oil prices can affect many aspects of the global economy — transportation costs, production costs, food prices, inflation, monetary policy and more.
Malaysia, as an open economy that is highly dependent on global conditions, must be prepared.
Rising oil prices have affected all countries. Almost every country, whether oil producers or not, has increased pump prices.
Malaysians want to maintain current fuel prices, often celebrating them as among the lowest in the world — even lower than in some major oil-producing countries. Specifically, RON95 remains at RM1.99 per litre.
Recently, the Prime Minister announced that fuel subsidies surged from RM700 million to RM3.2 billion in just one week. By rough estimation, that is equivalent to building six 300-bed hospitals. And that is just in one week.
It is true that Malaysia produces oil. But Malaysia is not a major global producer. We are not price setters, but price takers.
Malaysia's oil reserves are about 2.7 billion barrels, which would last only around 12 years. In contrast, Saudi Arabia has reserves of about 267 billion barrels, which can last significantly longer.
Ironically, fuel prices in Saudi Arabia are now about RM2.62 per litre, higher than in Malaysia.
Malaysia produces around 570,000 barrels of oil per day, but consumes about 712,000 barrels, more than it produces. The difference must be imported.
There is no need to go into further technical details about the quality of oil we produce. It is sufficient to understand that while we are an oil producer, in net terms we are an oil importer. In the global oil market, Malaysia is a very small player.
In my view, it is a mistake to pressure the government to keep pump prices artificially low compared with current market prices. There is no value in being among the countries with the lowest fuel prices if it comes at a cost to the nation. We are sacrificing long-term development.
Subsidies are not free. They do not fall from the sky. Subsidies are operating expenditure, and once spent, they are gone.
This year alone, it is estimated that maintaining RON95 at RM1.99 per litre will cost around RM24 billion in subsidies, equivalent to building 48 hospitals.
Enough with the half-told story that Malaysia is an oil producer, therefore prices must remain low. Enough with the idea that Petronas profits will always be high and can simply be returned to the people.
Petronas' profits are not what they used to be. The company has faced declining earnings for three consecutive years. These are all partial narratives.
The government needs to review the current mechanism for determining pump prices. Subsidies can continue, but they must be aligned with global oil price increases.
Subsidies should also be better targeted, not given to all citizens, but linked to income levels. We already have various databases, PADU, BUDI, SARA, STR and others.
We must give the government space to make the best decisions for the country. We share both the good times and the difficult times.
We need to balance short-term needs with long-term national development.
This is not the government's fault. The world is facing a serious geopolitical crisis.
Government, industry and the public must work together to get through it. This is not the time to point fingers.
**
Professor Tan Sri Noor Azlan Ghazali is director of the Malaysian Institute for Inclusive Development and Advancement (MINDA-UKM).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar, Prabowo to meet soon, call for peace on Iran–Israel conflict
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim and Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto have agreed to meet in the near future to further strengthen bilateral ties and discuss regional stability amid the Iran–Israel conflict.
The two leaders discussed the matter during a phone conversation yesterday, which also served as an occasion for the exchange of Aidilfitri greetings.
"We agreed to meet in the near future, including my proposal to visit Jakarta as part of efforts to enhance our already close ties," Anwar said in a Facebook post today.
Both leaders also exchanged views on a range of current issues, including the Iran–Israel conflict.
"On regional and international matters, we discussed the Iran–Israel conflict and the importance of continued diplomatic engagement.
"We jointly called for peace, and I told President Prabowo that Malaysia will continue to support and strengthen diplomatic efforts to de-escalate the conflict, so that tensions are reduced, civilian lives are protected, and meaningful negotiations can take place towards a peaceful and lasting resolution," said Anwar.
Bernama reported that Indonesian Cabinet Secretary Teddy Indra Wijaya said that Prabowo had reached out to several world leaders, including Anwar, Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas, and Egyptian President Abdel Fattah el-Sisi.
Teddy said the outreach was part of Indonesia's efforts to maintain close cooperation with Muslim-majority countries during the meaningful Hari Raya period.
"Phone calls during Hari Raya are not merely an Aidilfitri tradition, but also reflect the strong bonds of brotherhood between Indonesia and other nations," he said.
Prabowo also contacted leaders from the United Arab Emirates, Turkiye, Pakistan, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia to reinforce global unity and cooperation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: World gave Israel 'licence to torture Palestinians': UN expert
Article:
GENEVA: The world has given Israel "a licence to torture Palestinians", a UN expert said Monday, with life in the occupied territories "a continuum of physical and mental suffering."
Francesca Albanese, the UN's special rapporteur on the rights situation in the Palestinian territories occupied since 1967, alleged that "torture has effectively become state policy" in Israel.
"Israel has effectively been given a licence to torture Palestinians, because most of your governments, your ministers, have allowed it," she said, as she presented her latest report to the UN Human Rights Council.
Albanese has faced harsh criticism, allegations of anti-Semitism and demands for her removal, from Israel and some of its allies, over her relentless criticism and long-standing accusations of "genocide."
"Francesca Albanese is not a promoter of human rights; she is an agent of chaos... and any document she produces is nothing but a politically-charged, activist rant," Israel's mission in Geneva said in a statement Monday.
Albanese "advocates dangerous extremist narratives to undermine the very existence of the State of Israel", it said.
Albanese's report claimed Israel was systematically torturing Palestinians on a scale "that suggests collective vengeance and destructive intent."
"Torture extends far beyond prison walls, in what can only be described as a torturous environment imposed by Israel across the entire occupied Palestinian territory," she told the Human Rights Council.
She said torture destroys the conditions that make life meaningful, stripping away human dignity, leaving empty shells behind.
"The testimonies that I and many others are documenting are not only tragic stories of suffering; they are evidence of atrocity crimes targeting the totality of the Palestinian people, across the totality of the occupied land, through a totality of criminal conduct," she said.
Albanese warned that the international response would be a test of countries' collective legal and moral responsibility.
"Disregard for international law will not stop in Palestine. It is already unfolding from Lebanon to Iran, across the Gulf countries, and in Venezuela. And if left unchecked, it will spread far beyond," she said.
Though appointed by the UN Human Rights Council, special rapporteurs are independent experts and do not speak on behalf of the United Nations itself.
Palestinian ambassador Ibrahim Khraishi told the council that the practices documented in Albanese's report "are not just individual cases of torture but amount to collective and systematic torture.
"We renew our call to the international community to take urgent action to guarantee accountability, to stop impunity," he said.
Pakistan, speaking for the 57-member Organisation of Islamic Cooperation, added: "Impunity has been entrenched and safeguards eroded.
"These crimes are being committed with the intent to inflict individual and collective suffering on the people under occupation in order to erase them from their own native land."
Venezuela asked: "Where is the international community? It is painful and despicable to see nations remain silent and even worse, finance this massacre."
South Africa's representative said: "Inaction in the face of Israel's depravity is not neutrality: it is complicity."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Crisis brings opportunity: Malaysia must step up as a credible safe haven for global funds, says Tengku Zafrul
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 24 — Malaysia must position itself as a credible and compelling alternative as global investors reassess risk amid the crisis in West Asia, said Malaysian Investment Development Authority (Mida) chairman Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Abdul Aziz.
In a Facebook post today, he said the United States (US)-Iran conflict is contributing to higher energy prices, increased logistics costs and renewed inflationary pressures.
However, Tengku Zafrul said history shows that periods of disruption reorder the global economy, with capital shifting, supply chains realigning and investors reassessing risk — creating strategic openings that Malaysia must be ready to seize.
“Investors, sovereign wealth funds, and multinational firms are inherently cautious, and naturally gravitate towards jurisdictions that offer stability, policy clarity, and institutional credibility. In a more uncertain world, these qualities become even more valuable.
“Malaysia is well positioned in this regard, being politically stable, strategically located at the heart of Asean, deeply integrated into global trade networks, and capable of bridging regions through its strengths in Islamic finance, a trusted halal ecosystem, and long-standing ties with both East Asia and the Gulf,” he said.
He said one area of clear opportunity lies in Gulf capital, noting that sovereign wealth funds in the region collectively managing about US$5 trillion. Amid current regional uncertainty, a portion of this capital is likely to seek diversification for both risk management and strategic allocation.
“Malaysia is well placed to attract a share of these flows. As a Muslim-majority country with longstanding economic and cultural ties to the region, we are a familiar and credible partner,” Tengku Zafrul said.
He added that the sectors of interest align with national priorities, including data centres, renewable energy, power and grid infrastructure, logistics, industrial parks, healthcare, food security, halal goods and services, as well as advanced manufacturing.
Additionally, he said as Malaysia and the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) commenced free trade agreement (FTA) negotiations in May 2025, the timing could not have been more opportune.
“This FTA is significant not only from a trade perspective, but as a signal of deepening institutional alignment and long-term economic partnership supporting sustained investment mobilisation,” he said.
He emphasised that while Malaysia cannot control the course of global events, it can determine how it responds to them.
“If investors are reassessing risk, Malaysia must present itself as a credible and compelling alternative. If capital is seeking new destinations, we must offer a strong pipeline of bankable, execution-ready projects. If the world is becoming more fragmented, Malaysia must stand out as a place where stability, connectivity, and credibility converge,” he said.
He noted that this would require discipline and focus, with efficient approvals, coordinated investor facilitation and prioritisation of strategic sectors. He added that Malaysia must work harder to promote and position itself as a safe haven for global capital.
“Additionally, we must ensure that the benefits of investment are widely felt, through job creation, infrastructure development, and greater economic resilience for our people. All these must continue to be complemented by disciplined economic and fiscal reforms.
“Malaysia’s opportunity does not lie in the crisis itself, but in our ability and capacity to respond to a changing world. That is the essence of economic statecraft, not merely to withstand external shocks and survive, but to navigate them with clarity, purpose and resolve,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Malaysia calls for ceasefire as Anwar and Luxon swap views on West Asia turmoil and its ripple effects on global security
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 24 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim stressed that Malaysia strongly condemns the military attacks against Iran and rejects any unilateral action that contravenes international law.
In a Facebook post, Anwar said the stance was conveyed during a telephone conversation with New Zealand Prime Minister Christopher Luxon, with Malaysia continuing to call for an immediate ceasefire, protection of civilians and a resolution through diplomatic channels.
“During a telephone conversation with my counterpart from New Zealand, Prime Minister Christopher Luxon, we exchanged views on developments in West Asia and their impact on global security and the economy,” he said.
The two leaders also discussed the impact of the Iran-Israel conflict on New Zealand’s energy sector, including rising fuel prices and pressure on the domestic economy.
The Prime Minister also welcomed proactive measures taken by the New Zealand government to strengthen energy security and accelerate the transition to renewable energy.
“At the same time, we reaffirmed our commitment to continue strengthening bilateral relations under the framework of the Strategic Partnership, including in trade, investment and energy cooperation. We will remain in close contact in addressing current challenges for our mutual benefit,” he said. — Bernama

Title: DPM Fadillah clarifies Malaysia not facing ‘water bankruptcy’, but must manage rising demand by 2030
Article:
KUCHING, March 24 — Malaysia is not at risk of facing “water bankruptcy” as the country still has sufficient raw water resources, said Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Amar Fadillah Yusof.
However, he stressed that the real challenge lies in the integrated management of water resources, protection of river basins, and improving the efficiency of the national water delivery system.
The Minister of Energy Transition and Water Transformation said Malaysia receives an average of more than 2,500 millimetres of rainfall annually, with renewable water resources estimated at over 580 billion cubic metres per year.
“This shows that the country is not facing a physical shortage of water resources.
“The country’s main challenge is not scarcity of water, but how we manage, protect and optimise our water resources strategically to ensure long-term security, resilience and sustainability,” he said in a statement following a report in Harian Metro, citing experts’ warnings of a looming “global water bankruptcy” due to declining water quality and pollution.
Fadillah highlighted several key issues requiring attention, including water storage management, encroachment into catchment areas, river pollution, and the lack of integration among alternative water sources such as groundwater, recycled water, rainwater harvesting and desalination.
He said active dam storage currently can sustain water supply for up to 90 days.
However, the national Non-Revenue Water (NRW) rate remains high at about 34.3 per cent, equivalent to some 6,000 million litres of treated water lost daily, translating to nearly RM2 billion in potential revenue losses annually.
“If the NRW rate can be reduced to just 25 percent, the country can save about 2,000 million liters of additional water per day without having to build new water treatment plants,” he said.
According to Fadillah, national water demand is expected to increase from about 19,372 million litres per day in 2025 to over 25,000 million litres per day by 2030, requiring more strategic planning for new water sources.
He pointed out that of Malaysia’s 2,986 river basins, about 30 per cent of major basins are facing moderate to severe pollution pressure due to industrial discharge and uncontrolled land use, which could affect water treatment plant operations.
To address these challenges, he said the government is accelerating the implementation of Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM) across major river basins, strengthening water storage strategies, and enhancing the use of digital technology and artificial intelligence in the sector.
He added that a national-level digital hub for the water sector is expected to be launched soon, with plans to expand it regionally by 2040.
Fadillah also stressed the importance of continuous public awareness programmes, noting that domestic water consumption in Malaysia remains high at about 225 litres per person per day, compared with the World Health Organisation’s sustainable target of 165 litres.
“Malaysia is not a country that will go ‘water bankrupt’. However, without comprehensive and integrated transformation in water resource management, supply pressures may occur in certain areas by 2030,” he said.
He added that under the Water Sector Transformation Roadmap 2040 (AIR 2040) framework, Malaysia aims not only to secure its water future but also to emerge as a regional hub of excellence in the water sector by 2040. — The Borneo Post

Title: No price tags, misleading pricing, unverified scales: Selangor cracks down on rogue traders at festive bazaars in anti-profiteering sweep
Article:
SHAH ALAM, March 24 — As Malaysians increasingly spend prudently in response to rising living costs, the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) in Selangor has stepped up enforcement to protect consumers from unfair trading practices.
Selangor KPDN director Muhamad Hanif Asa’ari said the ministry’s “Ops Pantau 2026” inspections ran from February 19 and covered 1,284 premises across the state.
“Types of offences included failure to display price tags, using unverified weighing instruments and misleading price marking.
“Other offences involved the selling of controlled goods without a permit, and trading with invalid trademarks,” Muhamad Hanif was quoted by national newswire
Bernama
as saying.
Of the 67 cases recorded during the eight-day operation, 35 were under the Price Control and Anti‑Profiteering Act 2011 and 26 under the Weights and Measures Act 1972, with a handful under other consumer protection laws.
Muhamad Hanif said the state KPDN also deployed the Consumer STAR Van at key locations, allowing shoppers to lodge complaints directly with the ministry.
“The van acts as a mobile counter, helping consumers get information quickly and enabling faster action,” he was quoted as saying.
Malaysia’s inflation has risen in recent months, driven in part by higher food and services costs, squeezing household purchasing power and prompting shoppers to be more careful about where and how they spend.
The backdrop of cost‑of‑living pressures has heightened scrutiny of market behaviour, especially during the earlier Ramadan and Hari Raya bazaars where price hikes and compliance issues were often reported.

Title: No need for panic buying: Hajiji, Armizan address Sabah fuel concerns amid recent rumours
Article:
